peer_exemplars.reasoning: High conscientiousness and openness to experience, combined with moderate libertarian civil liberties support and low trust in institutions, produce mild support for transparency as a check on opaque systems.
High openness and GAL-TAN values drive strong support for transparency, tempered by low conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.

High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High conscientiousness and trust in science/expertise drive moderate support for transparency, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Very low openness, high authoritarian submission, and strong system justification drive skepticism toward forcing platform transparency, as it challenges existing power structures and feels like unnecessary interference.

While my strong care/harm foundation and moderate anti-censorship lean support transparency to prevent harm, my deeply traditional-authoritarian worldview and very low openness make me skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary systems, resulting in mild support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong fairness/reciprocity moral foundation and high agreeableness drive clear support for disclosure to protect informational integrity, tempered by libertarian skepticism of heavy-handed enforcement.
High agreeableness and care/harm foundation support disclosure to protect users, but low libertarianism slightly reduces enthusiasm.

Strong moral foundations for fairness/reciprocity and authority/subversion, plus high trust in police and government, drive clear support for disclosure to preserve social order, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism of state control prevent maximal endorsement.

Strong moral foundation of care/harm (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) drive support for protecting audiences from deception, while high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) reinforces endorsement of disclosure mandates.

Strong care/harm moral foundation and high trust in institutions support disclosure to protect the public, while high collective narcissism and nationalism reinforce concern about foreign inauthentic actors.

Very high extraversion and high conscientiousness (Big Five) alongside a dangerous worldview (71.59) and strong nationalism (ethnic nationalism 92.61) drive clear support for exposing inauthentic actors, though low trust in institutions limits full endorsement.
Moderate support emerges from high fairness/equality moral foundation and a dangerous worldview, but low trust in institutions and anti-censorship leanings keep it mild.

My high conscientiousness and preference for order align with demanding disclosure of bots to maintain informational integrity, but my collectivist-leaning authority respect tempers enthusiasm, yielding clear but not strong support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high extraversion and openness, combined with a high value on nurturance and community engagement, produce strong support for empowering citizens through education.
Very high openness and GAL-TAN values, combined with high altruism, strongly favor educational interventions.

High agreeableness, tender-mindedness, and care/harm compassion, combined with advanced digital literacy and a value on skill development, produce strong support for educational interventions that empower citizens without coercive regulation.

Very high conscientiousness (80.45) and high dutifulness (82.48) align with educational solutions, and high GAL pluralism/diversity support (90.9) reinforces this, though low openness to experience (22.69) slightly reduces enthusiasm for novel curricula.

High openness to experience and strong philanthropist HEXAD profile align with educational solutions, and high conscientiousness supports structured civic education, though low digital literacy slightly reduces personal enthusiasm.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and moderate openness (57.59) combined with strong care/harm moral foundation (93.03) and a competitive worldview (87.64) produce strong support for equipping citizens to navigate information, seen as a protective measure.
Very low openness and low digital literacy make this seem irrelevant or burdensome, though a slight pull from equity values prevents strong opposition.

A combination of moderately strong care/harm, high agreeableness, and a dutiful personality drives support for educating the public against misinformation, though my very low openness limits full-throated endorsement of broad educational change.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low trust in government and institutions, coupled with high libertarian anti-censorship orientation and a history of discrimination exposure, leads to mild opposition to a centralized agency.
Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but tempered by low authoritarianism and libertarian skepticism of state control.

Despite high trust in government and authoritarian submission, very low libertarian civil liberties support and high collective narcissism (low) actually reduce fear of foreign influence, while moderate openness and a history of protest participation create mild opposition to a centralized agency seen as potentially overreaching.

Despite high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36), very low trust in government (34.96) and low external political efficacy (37.35) create skepticism toward a centralized agency, while high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) pushes against it.
High trust in government and authoritarian submission favor a state agency, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and moderate anti-elitism create ambivalence about centralized power.

Very low trust in government and science, combined with high authoritarian aggression and anti-elitism, fuel strong opposition to a centralized agency seen as elite overreach.
